content:
# ANALYSIS OF ARCHIE’S PARAMETERS DETERMINATION TECHNIQUES

**Summary**: The thesis evaluates three different techniques—conventional, Core Archie Parameter Estimation (CAPE), and 3D determination—to calculate Archie's parameters (a, m, n) in carbonate rocks. It concludes that the CAPE and 3D methods provide more accurate water saturation values compared to the conventional method due to their ability to better account for complex pore geometries.

---

## Research Problem

The determination of accurate water saturation in carbonate reservoirs is complicated by the difficulty of accurately estimating Archie's parameters (a, m, n). Traditional methods often prioritize optimizing functions rather than actual water saturation values, leading to higher errors compared to newer techniques like CAPE and 3D methods.

## Objectives

- Conduct laboratory experiments to determine electrical properties of core plugs.
- Use appropriate algorithms to calculate Archie’s parameters and corresponding water saturation values by different techniques.
- Analyze and discuss experimental results to identify the most accurate technique for carbonate rocks.
- Evaluate the accuracy of four different techniques for determining Archie's parameters (a, m, n).
- Compare the resulting water saturation values across these methods using core samples from three wells (Well A, Well B, and Well C).
- Identify the most robust method for petrophysical interpretation in carbonate reservoirs.

## Methods

The study utilized a laboratory-based experimental approach. Core samples (carbonate) were prepared, cleaned with toluene and methanol, and dried. Porosity and permeability were measured using an overburden permeameter porosity meter (OPP 610). Brine was prepared to a specific salinity (79.339 g/l total dissolved solids) and its resistivity was measured using a Dresser Fann resistivity meter. Core samples were saturated with brine and desaturated using nitrogen in a porous plate cell (ER2005 system). Three calculation techniques were applied: 1) Conventional (based on log-log plots of formation factor vs. porosity), 2) CAPE (minimizing mean-square error between measured and calculated saturations), and 3) 3D Technique (using a three-dimensional regression analysis of water saturation, formation resistivity, and porosity). Statistical analyses including average percent relative error, root mean square error (RMSE), and correlation coefficients were used to evaluate the accuracy of each method.

## Data and Materials

44 core plug samples from 3 wells (Well A: 17; Well B: 15; Well C: 12). Samples included limestone, dolomite, and some with vugs. Synthetic brine prepared with NaCl, CaCl2, MgCl2·6H2O, Na2SO4, and NaHCO3.

## Key Findings

- The conventional method for determining Archie's parameters in carbonate rocks often leads to non-physical results or higher errors compared to CAPE and 3D methods. ([[2009-al-gathe|AL-GATHE, 2009, p. 33]])
- The CAPE technique provides a more 'natural' and physically meaningful way of averaging Archie’s parameters compared to the conventional method. ([[2009-al-gathe|AL-GATHE, 2009, p. 33]])
- The parameter 'a' (tortuosity factor) is often found to be a weak-fitting parameter with no significant physical meaning, frequently being set to unity in many models. ([[2009-al-gathe|AL-GATHE, 2009, p. 33]])
- In carbonate rocks, the cementation factor (m) varies significantly based on lithology and pore geometry; for example, vuggy or moldic systems often result in m values greater than 2. ([[2009-al-gathe|AL-GATHE, 2009, p. 23]])
- The 3D technique allows for the simultaneous determination of a, m, and n by treating the problem as a plane in three-dimensional space (log porosity, log water saturation, and log resistivity). ([[2009-al-gathe|AL-GATHE, 2009, p. 35]])
- Standard deviation and correlation coefficients were used to assess the quality of fit for the different determination techniques. ([[2009-al-gathe|AL-GATHE, 2009, p. 40]])
- The conventional method for well A yielded an average water saturation error of 14% (RMS 0.19), while the CAPE (a, m, n) method reduced this to 10% (RMS 0.13). ([[2009-al-gathe|AL-GATHE, 2009, p. 79]])
- The 3D Method produced a correlation factor of 0.92 for well A and 0.84 for well B, showing high consistency with measured values. ([[2009-al-gathe|AL-GATHE, 2009, p. 94]])
- In well C, the 3D Method showed a correlation factor of 0.941 and an average error of only 6.8%. ([[2009-al-gathe|AL-GATHE, 2009, p. 108]])
- The study found that the calculation of 'n' as a separate problem from 'a' and 'm' in conventional methods is not physically correct and leads to higher errors. ([[2009-al-gathe|AL-GATHE, 2009, p. 69]])
- The CAPE (a, m, n) method yielded the lowest absolute relative error (12.1%) compared to 15.3% for CAPE (1, m, n) and 17.7% for the conventional method. ([[2009-al-gathe|AL-GATHE, 2009, p. 119]])
- The 3-D method provided a correlation factor of 0.86, while the CAPE (a, m, n) method achieved the highest correlation factor of 0.87. ([[2009-al-gathe|AL-GATHE, 2009, p. 119]])
- The conventional method is the worst technique for determining Archie's parameters as it optimizes functions rather than water saturation values. ([[2009-al-gathe|AL-GATHE, 2009, p. 125]])
- CAPE (a, m, n) provides improved values of Archie's parameters from standard resistivity measurements on core samples compared to the conventional method. ([[2009-al-gathe|AL-GATHE, 2009, p. 125]])
- The 3-D and CAPE methods use all data points of Sw < 1.0, unlike the conventional method which ignores these values during determination of a and m. ([[2009-al-gathe|AL-GATHE, 2009, p. 125]])

## Limitations

The conventional method has significant limitations in carbonate rocks due to its sensitivity to pore geometry. The CAPE method requires multiple core samples to ensure convergence, and the 3D method's complexity can hinder adoption by some analysts.

## Recommendations and Future Work

For applications where the highest possible accuracy in hydrocarbon saturation is required, it is recommended to use the 3-D method, unless there are adverse conditions as mentioned in the thesis.
